If these courses accomplish only one thing, it's to enable you to get over your fear of negotiation so you're empowered to navigate the process with empathy. People often feel a conflict of negotiation so they avoid useful discussions. Out of a worry it will escalate into personal attacks they can't handle. People in close relationships often avoid making their own interests known and instead compromise across the board so they won't be perceived as greedy or self-interested. They stay silent, grow bitter, and grow apart. The first natural impulse for many is to avoid the tough negotiation, throw in the towel, and even run. The mere idea of clearly asserting what you want is dramatic because you will manufacture an extreme reaction from your counterpart in your head. We take ourselves hostage, we defeat ourselves before we even get to the table. We do the work of the combative adversary for them. Your human mind is naturally wired from the caveman days to be overly negative. We needed the caveman brain when the environment actually did contain sabre-toothed tigers that would eat us. The problem is the caveman brain is still there inside all of us. We envision 13 of the actual three negative consequences we might face. Think of how much upside, how much prosperity, how much beauty we're denying ourselves. That's why wimp-win deals are so common in the kitchen and the boardroom. Your sweaty palms adjust an expression of fear, a few trigger happy neurons, firing because of something more base or innate human desire to get along with other members of the tribe. It's not the person across the table who scares us, it's the conflict itself. In order to truly become great at anything, a great negotiator, a great manager, a great husband, a great wife, you're going to have to get over your fear of conflict. You're going to have to embrace regular, thoughtful conflict as the basis of effective negotiation and of life. Please remember that our emphasis throughout this course is that the adversary is the situation and that the person you appear to be in conflict with is actually your partner. Your goal should be to establish trust in order to work collaboratively on coming to an agreement. There's a significant amount of research that shows the genuine, honest conflict between people around their goals actually helps energize the problem-solving process in a collaborative way. Skilled negotiators have a talent for using conflict to keep the negotiation going without stumbling into a personal battle, pushing for what you believe isn't selfish and it isn't bullying. Your amygdala, the caveman part of the brain that processes fear, will try to convince you to give up to flee because you're the person who's right or you're being cruel or it's going to get ugly. But if you're an honest, decent person looking for reasonable outcome, you can overcome your amygdala's response. The style of negotiation that we've been teaching you is an information-obsessed, empathic search for the best possible deal. You're trying to uncover value, not strong arm or humiliate your counterpart. When you ask calibrated questions, you're leading your counterpart to your goals, but you're also leading them to examine and articulate what they want and why and how they can achieve it. You're provoking creativity from them and therefore pulling them towards a collaborative solution. Keep in mind that each person during a negotiation is in possession of about three to five black swans. When you uncover these, they change the entire course of the negotiation because they are the value multipliers. Now finding them is intrinsically difficult because we don't always know where and how the unknowns overlap. Your counterpart might not even realize how important a piece of information is. Keep probing, listening, gathering information, and eventually the black swans will be revealed. Let your known knowns guide you but not blind you. Each case is new, so remain flexible and adaptable. Yet every case has common types of threads. These threads lead you to the unknowns, the black swans. Review everything you hear with your counterpart. You won't hear everything the first time. That's the beauty of paraphrases, mirrors, and summaries. There are automatic reviews with your counterpart that trigger more information. Compare notes with team members and use backup listeners whose job is to listen between the lines. I guarantee you they will catch things you miss. Work to understand the other side's religion. Digging into worldviews inherently implies moving beyond the negotiating table to the life, emotional, and otherwise of your counterpart. That's where you'll uncover black swans also. And before you assume that someone is being crazy or rational, remember that they might just have bad information, constraints, or hidden desires. Your job is to search and uncover these hidden factors. Please bear in mind that when discovering black swans, there's more information to be had sitting face to face. Emails almost always give the wrong tone of voice. Pay special attention to your counterpart's teammates, verbal and non-verbal communications when they feel your focus is off of them. Before we move into the scenario for this course, I want to leave you with one request. No matter what setting you're in, whether it's an office or around the dinner table, don't avoid honest, clear differences. And don't plow into them without using tactical empathy first. That's how you get the best price for your car, the raise you desire, the contract you deserve, and the relationship you want. It will also help you with marriage, friendships, and your family. You can only become an exceptional negotiator and a great person by both listening and speaking clearly and empathically, by treating counterparts and yourself with dignity and respect, and most of all, by being honest about what you want and what you can and cannot do. Every negotiation, every conversation, every moment of your life holds a series of small conflicts that, when managed well, can rise to creative beauty. I ask you to embrace them.
